one_line_takeaway: The paper proves via a chi-squared PAC-Bayes bound that MIA-based auditing of machine unlearning has an irreducible 'alignment bias' between attacker training distribution and true unlearned-sample distribution, and replaces it with SMI/SMI-M, training-free statistical mixture-ratio estimators that beat seven MIA baselines (RULI, LiRA, RMIA, EMIA, IAM, Unleak, Random) on rho* accuracy across CIFAR-10/100 and CINIC-10 while eliminating shadow-model training cost (930-4302ms per audit vs 2.46x10^6ms per shadow model).
key_finding: On the full-retraining benchmark (Table 1), SMI-M achieved the best rho* estimation accuracy across all six reported dataset/model/unlearning-percentage settings, e.g. ResNet18-CIFAR10-5%: SMI-M rho*=92.44% vs SMI 88.89%, RULI 87.94%, IAM 88.67%, RMIA 66.68%, EMIA 39.10%, LiRA 87.84%, Unleak 62.58%, Random 50.06%; ResNet50-CIFAR100-10%: SMI-M 93.95% vs SMI 83.46%, RULI 74.44%, LiRA 87.38%, RMIA 60.74%; VIT-CINIC10-10%: SMI-M 93.62% vs SMI 91.44%, RULI 89.84%, IAM 41.01%, RMIA 15.33%. Among MIA baselines, RULI and LiRA were generally the strongest, and the paper notes RULI and LiRA achieved FPR of 0.00% in several settings while still diverging from each other in FNR (e.g., ResNet18-CIFAR100-5%: RULI FNR=15.60% vs LiRA FNR=10.47%, yet RULI's rho* of 83.00% was closer to the retrain target than LiRA's 86.64% in that row), which the authors cite as direct evidence that low FNR/FPR does not guarantee accurate unlearning auditing. On compute cost (Table 2, CIFAR-100), SMI took 930.71ms and SMI-M took 4301.66ms per full audit (200 bootstrap iterations, no shadow models), versus training a single LiRA-style shadow model at 2.46x10^6 ms and per-audit MIA runtimes of 1464.51ms (LiRA) to 5672.83ms (RULI), each still requiring 8 pre-trained shadow models. SMI/SMI-M rho* stabilized once the audited sample count reached roughly 200 (about 20 samples/class for a 10-class dataset; Fig. 4), and was insensitive to bootstrap count beyond a small number of iterations (Fig. 5). For SMI-M's kernel ablation (Table 3), the standard RBF kernel with median bandwidth was best (89.84% at CIFAR100-5%, 91.84% at CIFAR100-10%), while a 0.25x-bandwidth RBF kernel collapsed badly (24.39% and 26.03%). On practical (non-retraining) unlearning algorithms (Table 4), SMI-M-estimated forgetting levels ranked Retrain (89.84-93.95%) > Scrub (62.43-77.58%) > FineTune (55.42-68.66%) > Fisher (32.51-44.98%) across all eight dataset/backbone combinations, consistent with Scrub being identified as the strongest approximate-unlearning method.
core_contribution: The paper (titled in the actual PDF as 'SMI: Statistical Membership Inference for Reliable Unlearned Model Auditing', arXiv:2602.01150v2) argues that Membership Inference Attack (MIA)-based auditing of machine unlearning is structurally unreliable: a sample that evades an MIA classifier is not necessarily truly forgotten, because unlearned-sample features occupy a distributionally distinct region of feature space from genuine non-member features (the paper formalizes this as an 'alignment bias'). The authors prove this bias is unavoidable and unobservable via a chi-squared PAC-Bayes bound (Theorem 2.1, Corollary 2.2) showing MIA auditing error contains an irreducible term controlled by the Renyi divergence between the attacker's training distribution and the true unlearned-sample distribution. They then reframe unlearning auditing as an optimal-transport/Monge problem in feature space, relax it to estimating a mixture proportion rho (the fraction of unlearned-sample features that have become non-member-like), and propose Statistical Membership Inference (SMI) and a kernel-embedding variant SMI-M: training-free, shadow-model-free estimators of rho based on matching low-order moments (SMI: mean/covariance matching solved via a cubic equation, Lemma 3.2) or kernel mean embeddings/MMD (SMI-M, a convex quadratic program, Lemma 3.3). A bootstrap resampling procedure over the audited feature set produces a {5th,95th} percentile reference range for auditing reliability, replacing the shadow-model ensembles used by conventional MIA.
threat_model: This is an auditing/evaluation-methodology paper rather than a paper introducing a new attack against a victim system. The 'threat' framing is inverted from typical MIA papers: the auditor plays the role of a defender/regulator trying to verify that an unlearning algorithm has genuinely removed a training subset Du (right-to-be-forgotten compliance), while conventional MIA-based auditors are shown to be exploitable/misleading because a poorly-calibrated or insufficiently-trained MIA classifier can produce 'illusory forgetting' (false negatives interpreted as successful unlearning). The paper also notes, in an appendix discussion (Q&A / limitations section), that a malicious actor performing adversarial-example-based unlearning forgery causes the SMI estimate rho* to diverge far outside [0,1] (rho* >> 1 in their stated counterexample), which they present as a detectable anomaly signal rather than a demonstrated attack vector with quantitative evasion results.
limitations: The paper's own appendix (Discussion and Limitations, Appendix B) states explicitly: SMI does not use shadow models, so it lacks the auxiliary signal shadow-model MIA exploits and its single-sample auditing performance is weaker than MIA; the authors state empirically that at least ~20 samples per class are needed for an effective audit. The core mixture-proportion relaxation (Fu approximately (1-rho)Fm + rho*Fn) is an approximation to the true Monge transport problem and the paper acknowledges it does not always hold -- they construct a counterexample where an adversarial-example-based forgery of the unlearning process drives rho* far outside [0,1], meaning SMI can fail (though they argue an out-of-range rho* is itself detectable as an anomaly). They also tried a Wasserstein-distance variant (SMI-W) as a theoretically more natural fit to the Monge formulation but report it underperformed the simpler SMI/SMI-M estimators, without providing a full explanatory theory. SMI-M is roughly 4.6x slower than SMI due to kernel-embedding computation. Evaluation is confined to image classification (CIFAR-10/100, CINIC-10 with ResNet/ViT); no language-model or other modality unlearning setting is evaluated in the main text (as visible in the extracted pages).

quantitative_results: Table 1, ResNet18-CIFAR10-5%-64: SMI-M rho*=92.44%, SMI rho*=88.89%, RULI rho*=87.94%, IAM rho*=88.67%, RMIA rho*=66.68%, EMIA rho*=39.10%, LiRA rho*=87.84%, Unleak rho*=62.58%, Random rho*=50.06%
Table 1, ResNet50-CIFAR100-10%-32: SMI-M rho*=93.95%, SMI rho*=83.46%, RULI rho*=74.44%, IAM rho*=64.98%, RMIA rho*=60.74%, EMIA rho*=69.94%, LiRA rho*=87.38%, Unleak rho*=4.68%, Random rho*=54.14%
Table 1, VIT-CINIC10-10%-16: SMI-M rho*=93.62%, SMI rho*=91.44%, RULI rho*=89.84%, IAM rho*=41.01%, RMIA rho*=15.33%, EMIA rho*=36.66%, LiRA rho*=83.51%, Unleak rho*=55.71%, Random rho*=51.04%

Table 2 (compute cost, CIFAR-100): SMI=930.71ms, SMI-M=4301.66ms (both with 200 bootstrap iters, no shadow models); LiRA=1464.51ms with 8 shadow models; RULI=5672.83ms with 8 shadow models; training one shadow model=2.46e6 ms
Figure 4: SMI/SMI-M rho* estimates converge/stabilize at roughly 200 audited samples (~20 per class for a 10-class dataset)

Table 3 (SMI-M kernel ablation, CIFAR100-5% / CIFAR100-10%): standard RBF median-bandwidth = 89.84%/91.84% (best), RBF(0.25x) = 24.39%/26.03% (worst), Laplacian = 80.05%/82.41%, Linear = 86.65%/89.88%
Table 4 (practical unlearning, SMI-M rho* range across 8 backbone/dataset settings): Retrain 89.84-93.95%, Scrub 62.43-77.58%, FineTune 55.42-68.66%, Fisher 32.51-44.98%
